Home.title: Get Your Birth Certificate
Home.subtitle: Free guide to ordering your birth certificate in any US state or territory. For voting, passports, REAL ID, and more.
Home.hasId: I have a government-issued photo ID
Home.hasIdDesc: Driver's license, passport, or military ID. Get your options in 30 seconds.
Home.noId: I don't have a photo ID, or I need help
Home.noIdDesc: No ID, can't afford fees, complicated situation? We'll guide you step by step.
Home.whyTitle: Why You Might Need a Birth Certificate
Home.voteDesc: Many states now require photo ID to vote. A birth certificate is the first step to getting that ID.
Home.passportDesc: A certified birth certificate is required for your first US passport application.
Home.realIdDesc: Now required for domestic flights. Your birth certificate is the key document you need.
Home.barriersSubtitle: Millions of Americans struggle to get their birth certificate. Our guided path helps with:
Home.footerDisclaimer: This is a free, open-source tool. We are not affiliated with any government agency.
Home.footerPrivacy: We do not collect, store, or sell your personal information. All processing happens in your browser. We use anonymous, cookie-free analytics to count page views.

BornAbroad.title: Born Outside the United States
BornAbroad.subtitle: If you are a US citizen born abroad, your birth was not recorded by a US state. You need a different document.
BornAbroad.crbaTitle: You Need a Consular Report of Birth Abroad (CRBA)
BornAbroad.crbaDesc: If your birth was registered with a US embassy or consulate, you can request a replacement FS-240 or DS-1350 from the US Department of State.
BornAbroad.fee: Fee: $50 for a replacement Consular Report of Birth Abroad
BornAbroad.neverRegistered: If your birth was never registered with a US consulate, you may need to apply for a Certificate of Citizenship (Form N-600) through USCIS
BornAbroad.parentsCitizens: If one or both parents were US citizens at the time of your birth, you may still qualify — contact the nearest US embassy
BornAbroad.notSureDesc: If you were born in a US state or territory (including Puerto Rico, Guam, US Virgin Islands, American Samoa, or Northern Mariana Islands), your birth certificate comes from that jurisdiction, not the State Department.

Results.stepsWithId: {count} steps to get your birth certificate.
Results.stepsWithoutId: {count} steps to get your birth certificate and a state ID.
Results.nearestOfficeDesc: Based on your zip code, this is your local office. In-person requests are often processed same-day.
Results.countyNoDetails: We found {county} County for your zip code, but don't have office details yet. Contact the {county} County Clerk/Recorder for in-person service.
Results.disclaimer: This information is provided as a guide only. We are not affiliated with any government agency. Requirements and fees may change — verify with your state's vital records office before submitting your application.

Results.notFoundCdcDesc: Find your state's vital records office
Results.notFoundUsagovDesc: Federal guidance on getting your birth certificate

Plan.bornAbroadTitle: You May Need a Consular Report of Birth, Not a State Certificate
Plan.bornAbroadDesc: If you are a US citizen born outside the United States, your birth was not recorded by any US state. Instead, you need a Consular Report of Birth Abroad (FS-240 or DS-1350) from the US Department of State.
Plan.bornAbroadTip1: If your birth was registered with a US embassy or consulate, request a replacement FS-240 from the US Department of State.
Plan.bornAbroadTip2: Apply online at travel.state.gov or call 1-888-407-4747.
Plan.bornAbroadTip3: Fee: $50 for a replacement Consular Report of Birth Abroad.
Plan.bornAbroadTip4: If your birth was never registered with a US consulate, you may need to apply for a Certificate of Citizenship (Form N-600) through USCIS.
Plan.bornAbroadTip5: If one or both parents were US citizens at the time of your birth, you may still qualify for a CRBA — contact the nearest US embassy.
Plan.bornBeforeTitle: Your Birth May Predate State Records
Plan.bornBeforeDesc: {state} state records begin in {year}. If you were born before then, your birth record may not be in the state system.
Plan.bornBeforeTip1: Contact the county clerk or registrar in the county where you were born — they may have earlier records.
Plan.bornBeforeTip2: You may need to file for a 'delayed birth certificate,' which requires supporting evidence (census records, hospital records, baptismal records, school records, family bibles, affidavits from relatives).
Plan.bornBeforeTip3: Contact {office} at {phone} for guidance on delayed registration.
Plan.noIdDesc: Contact {office} at {phone} to discuss options for requesting a birth certificate without a government-issued photo ID. Many states accept alternative forms of identification.
Plan.noIdTip1: This is the most common barrier — you're not alone.
Plan.noIdTip2: Ask specifically about alternative verification options.
Plan.noIdTip3: A social worker, homeless services provider, or legal aid organization may be able to help vouch for your identity.
Plan.noIdTip4: Some states allow a notarized statement from someone who can verify your identity.
Plan.feeWaivedTitle: Your Birth Certificate Fee Is Waived
Plan.feeWaiverTitle: You May Qualify for a Fee Waiver
Plan.feeWaiverTip1: Apply for the fee waiver BEFORE submitting your application to avoid paying unnecessarily.
Plan.feeWaiverTip2: Keep documentation of your eligibility (e.g., letter from shelter, VA documentation).
Plan.nameChangeDesc: Since your name has changed from what appears on your birth certificate, you'll need documentation linking your current legal name to your birth name.
Plan.nameChangeTip1: Acceptable documents: marriage certificate, divorce decree, court order for name change.
Plan.nameChangeTip2: You may need certified copies of these documents — not photocopies.
Plan.genderMarkerTitle: Updating the Gender Marker on Your Birth Certificate
Plan.genderMarkerDesc: The process to amend the gender marker on a birth certificate varies significantly by state. Some states allow it with a simple form, others require a court order, and a few do not allow changes.
Plan.genderMarkerTip2: The National Center for Transgender Equality (transequality.org/documents) maintains a state-by-state guide to updating identity documents.
Plan.genderMarkerTip3: Lambda Legal (lambdalegal.org) can provide legal assistance if you encounter difficulties.
Plan.genderMarkerTip4: Note: you may want to get your current birth certificate first, then apply for the amendment as a separate step.
Plan.adoptedDesc: As an adopted person, you will receive your amended birth certificate (showing your adoptive parents). Access to your original birth certificate varies by state.
Plan.adoptedTip1: Contact {office} at {phone} for information on adoptee birth certificate access in {state}.
Plan.adoptedTip2: Organizations like the Adoptee Rights Law Center (adopteerightslaw.com) can provide state-specific guidance.
Plan.homelessDesc: Several organizations can help you obtain your birth certificate at no cost and assist with the ID catch-22.
Plan.homelessTip1: Contact a local homeless services provider or shelter — they may be able to request your certificate on your behalf.
Plan.homelessTip2: Spread the Vote (spreadthevote.org) helps people without ID in 20 states.
Plan.homelessTip3: VoteRiders (voteriders.org) provides free assistance with ID and birth certificates for voting purposes.
Plan.homelessTip4: Ask about the SchoolhouseConnection 'I Am Here' legal hotline for youth.
Plan.incarceratedTitle: Requesting a Birth Certificate While Incarcerated
Plan.incarceratedDesc: You can request your birth certificate from a correctional facility, though the process may take longer. Many facilities have social workers or reentry coordinators who can help.
Plan.incarceratedTip1: Ask your facility's social worker, case manager, or reentry coordinator for help with the application.
Plan.incarceratedTip2: Some states allow the facility to submit the request on your behalf.
Plan.incarceratedTip3: Legal aid organizations that serve incarcerated people can often assist — ask your facility's law library for contacts.
Plan.incarceratedTip4: Contact {office} at {phone} to ask about their process for incarcerated applicants.
Plan.incarceratedTip5: Many reentry programs include birth certificate assistance — ask about available programs before release.
Plan.downloadFormTitle: Download and Complete the Application Form
Plan.downloadFormDesc:
Download the official {state} birth certificate application form and fill it out completely.

You'll need to know:
• Your full name at birth (before any name changes)
• Date of birth
• City and county of birth
• Mother's full maiden name
• Father's full name (if listed on the certificate)
• Your current mailing address
• Purpose for requesting the certificate
Plan.downloadFormTip1: Fill out every field — incomplete applications cause delays.
Plan.downloadFormTip2: If you're unsure of exact details (like county of birth), enter your best guess — the office can often look it up.
Plan.downloadFormTip3: If your name has changed, include the name change documentation you gathered.
Plan.gatherDocsDescWithId: You'll need one of the following: {ids}. Make a clear photocopy to include with your application.
Plan.gatherDocsDescWithoutId: Gather the alternative ID documents described above (you'll need at least two). Also prepare your payment and completed application form.
Plan.gatherDocsTip1: Photocopies must be legible — if the copy is blurry, your application may be rejected.
Plan.submitTitle: Choose How to Submit Your Request
Plan.submitDesc: You have your documents ready. Now pick the method that works best for you:
Plan.submitTip1: Consider ordering 2–3 certified copies — many processes require an original certified copy, not a photocopy.
Plan.submitTip2: Store your birth certificate in a safe, dry place (fireproof safe or safe deposit box).
Plan.ssCardTitle: Next: Get a Replacement Social Security Card
Plan.ssCardDesc: You'll need your Social Security card for the next step (getting a state ID). Now that you have your birth certificate, you can request a replacement SS card from the Social Security Administration.
Plan.ssCardTip1: Visit your local Social Security office with your birth certificate. Find your nearest office at ssa.gov/locator.
Plan.ssCardTip2: You can also apply online at ssa.gov/myaccount if you can create an account.
Plan.ssCardTip3: A replacement Social Security card is FREE — there is no fee.
Plan.ssCardTip4: You're allowed up to 3 replacement cards per year and 10 in your lifetime.
Plan.ssCardTip5: Processing takes 10–14 business days by mail. Some offices can verify your number on the spot.
Plan.getStateIdDesc: Once you have both your birth certificate and Social Security card, you're ready for the final step.
Plan.afterReceiveTitle: After You Receive Your Birth Certificate
Plan.inPersonDescWithId: Bring your completed application, ID, and payment.
Plan.inPersonDescWithoutId: Going in person lets you talk to staff about alternative ID options.
Plan.inPersonTipNoId: Best option when you don't have a photo ID — staff can help you find a solution.
Plan.inPersonTipCounty: This is your local office based on your zip code. The {office} also accepts requests.
Plan.onlineDescVitalchek: Order through VitalChek, an authorized online vendor.
Plan.onlineTipFee: VitalChek charges a {fee} service fee on top of the state fee.
Plan.onlineTipVendors: Other authorized vendors may also be available.
Plan.mailDescWithId: Mail your completed application, a photocopy of your ID, and payment.
Plan.mailDescWithoutId: Mail your completed application, alternative ID documentation, and payment.
Plan.mailTipCertified: Send by certified mail so you have a tracking number.
Plan.mailTipCallFirst: Call first to confirm what alternative documentation they accept by mail.
Plan.acceptableIdLink: See {state}'s full list of acceptable IDs
Plan.findLocalOffice: Find a local office near you
Plan.nextVoterIdNoId:
You're building your way to a voter ID:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. Social Security card — visit your local SSA office (free)
3. State photo ID — visit your DMV with your birth certificate, Social Security card, and proof of address (utility bill, bank statement, or shelter letter)
Plan.nextVoterIdWithId:
Next steps to get your voter ID:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. Visit your state's DMV or ID-issuing office with your birth certificate, Social Security card, and proof of address
Plan.nextRealIdNoId:
You're building your way to a REAL ID:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. Social Security card — visit your local SSA office (free)
3. REAL ID — visit your DMV with your birth certificate, Social Security card, and two proofs of address
Plan.nextRealIdWithId:
Next steps to get your REAL ID:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. Visit your state's DMV with your birth certificate, Social Security card, and two proofs of address
Plan.nextPassportNoId:
You can apply for a US passport using your birth certificate — no state ID required:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. Find your nearest passport acceptance facility at travel.state.gov
3. Bring your birth certificate, a passport photo, and a witness who can verify your identity
Plan.nextPassportWithId:
Next steps to get your passport:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. Find your nearest passport acceptance facility at travel.state.gov
3. Bring your birth certificate, a passport photo, and proof of identity
Plan.nextEmploymentNoId:
Your birth certificate is a List C document for Form I-9 employment verification:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. Social Security card (also List C) — visit your local SSA office (free)
3. Check with your employer about accepted document combinations
Plan.nextEmploymentWithId: Your certified birth certificate can be used as a List C document for Form I-9 employment verification. Bring it to your employer along with other required documents.
Plan.nextBenefitsNoId:
You can now use your birth certificate to apply for benefits:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. For Social Security benefits — bring your birth certificate to your local SSA office
3. For Medicaid and other programs — contact the specific agency to confirm what documents they need
Plan.nextBenefitsWithId: You can now use your birth certificate to apply for benefits. Contact the specific agency (Social Security, Medicaid, etc.) to confirm what additional documents they require.
Plan.nextDefaultNoId:
You now have your birth certificate — a critical foundation document. From here:

1. Birth certificate — done!
2. Social Security card — visit ssa.gov or your local SSA office (free)
3. State ID — visit your DMV with your birth certificate and Social Security card
4. Passport — apply at travel.state.gov with your birth certificate and photo ID
Plan.nextDefaultWithId: Keep your certified birth certificate in a safe place. It can be used for many purposes including ID applications, passport applications, benefits enrollment, and more.
Plan.crossLinkMarriageCert: Need your marriage certificate too? Use our free Marriage Certificate Navigator.
Plan.crossLinkMarriageCertNameChange: If your name changed due to marriage, you'll need your marriage certificate to link your birth name to your current name.
Plan.crossLinkMarriageCertPassport: If your name changed due to marriage, you'll also need your marriage certificate for your passport application.
Plan.crossLinkMarriageCertRealId: If your name changed due to marriage, you'll also need your marriage certificate for REAL ID.
